turns.content: Imagine I am building a custom container in std::collections that holds generic data but doesn't own it, similar to how Vec<T> uses PhantomData. My goal is to prevent the compiler from allowing unsafe code where data might outlive its type, so I want to ensure dropck checks T properly without accidentally creating dangling references. I thought simply using PhantomData<T> would be enough to satisfy this requirement.

The misconception there is that PhantomData<T> alone guarantees safe lifetime checking for all scenarios; it only tells the compiler that your type does not hold any data itself and leaves the responsibility of tracking lifetimes entirely up to you. If you need the compiler to explicitly verify that T outlives your wrapper, you must use #[may_dangle] to opt out of dropck checks or employ PhantomData<fn(T) -> T> when you want invariance without ownership while still maintaining strict lifetime guarantees. In practice, for a custom collection where you control the drop logic, defining the trait impl Drop on your struct alongside PhantomData<T> ensures that the compiler knows exactly which types are being dropped and prevents accidental dangling references.

That makes sense regarding the invariance aspect, but I am confused about why PhantomData<T> doesn't automatically enforce the same strictness as PhantomData<fn(T) -> T>. If I want to avoid any risk of data outliving its type, shouldn't the former be sufficient since it already signals that no actual data is stored? Can you clarify how these two patterns differ in their ability to prevent dangling references?

PhantomData<T> and PhantomData<fn(T) -> T> serve different purposes in Rust's type system: while PhantomData<T> indicates your struct does not contain any fields of T, it does not inherently enforce strict lifetime checking unless combined with other traits; the latter is specifically designed to signal that your type should behave as if it owned its generic parameter without actually storing ownership. When you use PhantomData<fn(T) -> T>, you are telling the compiler that T must outlive your wrapper regardless of whether you explicitly implement Drop, which prevents dangling references more rigorously than simply declaring no data fields. This distinction is critical when building custom collections where you want to ensure dropck never fails due to accidental lifetime violations.
